{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} [[File:Osake-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|鳥居}}]] '''大酒神社'''（おおさけじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|京都府|Q120730}}{{jalink|京都市|Q34600}}{{jalink|右京区|Q580186}}太秦蜂岡町にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}。 === 祭神 === 祭神は次の5柱境内説明板。。 ; 主祭神 :* {{jalink|秦|Q34756}}{{jalink|始皇帝|Q7192}} :* {{jalink|弓月君|Q11066250|弓月王}}（ゆずきのきみ） :* {{jalink|秦酒公|Q54867522}}（はたのさけのきみ） ; 相殿神 :* 兄媛命（えひめのみこと） - 呉服女。 :* 弟媛命（おとひめのみこと） - 漢織女。 『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}での祭神の記載は1座。同帳では「大酒神社{{sub| 元名大辟神}}」とあることから、元の神名は「'''大辟神'''」であったことが知られる{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 大酒神について、{{jalink|広隆寺|Q3076920}}の{{jalink|縁起|Q522046}}である『広隆寺来由記』（山城州葛野郡楓野大堰郷広隆寺来由記、{{jalink|明応|Q1193841}}8年（{{jalink|1499年|Q6708}}）成立）[{{NDLDC|1879799/384}} 『群書類従 新校 第十八巻』]（内外書籍、1931年-1937年、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）384-388コマ。では{{jalink|秦|Q34756}}の{{jalink|始皇帝|Q7192}}の祖神とし、日本に渡来した{{jalink|功満王}}（秦{{jalink|始皇帝|Q7192}}の後裔、{{jalink|秦氏|Q987290}}遠祖）が{{jalink|勧請}}したとする{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。伝承自体の真偽は明らかでないが、伝承に見えるように古くから太秦一帯を開発した秦氏の氏神であったと推測され{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}、史書に見える秦酒公（はたのさけのきみ）と大酒神を関連づける説もある。一方{{jalink|近世|Q5308718}}の『{{jalink|雍州府志|Q18460389}}』では祭神を{{jalink|物部守屋|Q2424736}}とし、一説に{{jalink|秦河勝|Q5680877}}とする{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。また『{{jalink|都名所図会|Q11643495}}』では祭神を{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602}}・{{jalink|八幡神|Q261637}}・天満天神とし、別説に秦の始皇帝とする説や、秦河勝とする説を載せる{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 これらとは別に近年の研究では、元の神名の「大辟（大避）」から、「災厄を避ける（遠ざける）」いわゆる{{jalink|道祖神|Q269515}}とする説が挙げられている{{Sfn|大酒神社（神々）|1986年}}。この説では、『空華日工集』・『広隆寺縁起』において{{jalink|神体|Q327532}}が石と記されることと、一般に石神の多くが{{jalink|サルタヒコ|Q3090037|猿田彦神}}（道祖神）とされることとが関連づけて指摘される{{Sfn|大酒神社（神々）|1986年}}。一方、「オホサケ」を「大裂」の意とし、土木技術によって大地・川を裂き開拓を行なった秦氏をたたえる神格とする説もある{{jalink|水谷千秋|Q11549157}} 『謎の渡来人秦氏（文春新書734）』 文藝春秋、2009年、pp. 55-59。{{jalink|菅澤庸子|Q45819933}} {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/documents/1296808791808.pdf 「広隆寺」]}}『[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/yukari06.html 人権 ゆかりの地をたずねて 京都市内編]』 京都人権啓発推進会議、2001年、pp. 26-27（リンクは京都府ホームページ）。。 このように古代の祭神の神格は不詳ながら、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}16年（{{jalink|1883年|Q7816}}）の神社明細帳では上記5柱が祭神として記され、現在に継承されている{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 === 歴史 === === 創建 === [[File:Kouryuji Roumon.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|{{jalink|広隆寺|Q3076920}}}}]] 創建に関して『広隆寺来由記』では、{{jalink|仲哀天皇|Q179971}}（第14代）の時に渡来した{{jalink|功満王}}（秦始皇帝の後裔、弓月君の父親）が「秦始皇之祖神」を{{jalink|勧請}}したことに始まるとする{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 現在は{{jalink|広隆寺|Q3076920}}の東隣に小祠として鎮座するが、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}の{{jalink|神仏分離|Q1970535}}以前には広隆寺の桂宮院内に鎮座する{{jalink|伽藍神|Q10885799}}であった{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 === 概史 === {{jalink|六国史|Q1151168|国史}}では、{{jalink|嘉祥|Q1373157}}2年（{{jalink|849年|Q23902}}）に{{jalink|山城国|Q749276}}{{jalink|葛野郡|Q13579879}}の「大辟神」が霊験のある社であるとして従五位下の{{jalink|神階|Q11591025}}に叙せられた旨が記されている{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 {{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}では山城国葛野郡に「大酒神社 小 元名大辟神」として、{{jalink|式内社}}に列するとともに元々は「大辟神」の神名である旨が記載されている{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 『広隆寺来由記』では、「大酒大明神」の神階が順次昇叙され、{{jalink|治暦|Q934365}}4年（{{jalink|1068年|Q19421}}）には正一位の極位に達した旨が記されている{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。また同記では、{{jalink|延喜|Q1374325}}年間（{{jalink|901年|Q23580}}-{{jalink|923年|Q30108}}）に広隆寺に{{jalink|勧請}}された鎮守38所の1所として「大酒{{sub| 正一位}}」と見える{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 {{jalink|南北朝時代 (日本)|Q264012|南北朝時代}}の『空華日工集』以降、文献では広隆寺桂宮院の鎮守社として見え、広隆寺とともに推移した{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 {{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}後、{{jalink|神仏分離|Q1970535}}によって現在地に移転し{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534}}では{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}に列した{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 === 神階 === * 六国史時代における{{jalink|神階|Q11591025}}奉叙の記録 ** {{jalink|嘉祥|Q1373157}}2年（{{jalink|849年|Q23902}}）9月16日、従五位下 （『{{jalink|続日本後紀|Q1041301}}』）『続日本後紀』嘉祥2年（849年）9月丙寅（16日）条。 - 表記は「大辟神」。 * 六国史以後 ** {{jalink|朱雀天皇|Q361019}}年間（{{jalink|930年|Q23409}}-{{jalink|946年|Q23893}}）、従三位 （『広隆寺来由記』） ** {{jalink|天慶|Q1327340}}4年（{{jalink|941年|Q24255}}）5月15日、正三位 （『広隆寺来由記』） ** {{jalink|天喜|Q1193591}}3年（{{jalink|1055年|Q19371}}）11月20日、正二位 （『広隆寺来由記』） ** {{jalink|治暦|Q934365}}4年（{{jalink|1068年|Q19421}}）4月25日、正一位 （『広隆寺来由記』） === 摂末社 === 『式内社調査報告』では次の4社が境内社として記載されている{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 * 木枯神社 *: 祭神は大国主命。『{{jalink|日本三代実録|Q1052981}}』{{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|貞観}}4年（{{jalink|862年|Q24993}}）条『日本三代実録』貞観4年（862年）4月24日条。の「木枯神」に比定される{{jalink|国史見在社|Q118304363}}（異説あり）「木枯神」の比定地には、別に{{jalink|藤原順子|Q11623640}}（{{jalink|仁明天皇|Q349271}}女御）の東五条院（現・京都市下京区）内とする説がある {{Harv|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。{{Sfn|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。『広隆寺来由記』では「木枯明神」と見え、{{jalink|薬師如来|Q854773}}像を広隆寺に迎えた際、向日明神が広隆寺門前の槻の木を枯らしたが、この神を「木枯明神」として別に祀ると槻の木は蘇ったという{{Sfn|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。現在の大酒神社境内への遷座時期は詳らかでないが、『山城名勝志』でも広隆寺の大門南の民家後園に鎮座すると見えることから、それ以降のことと推測される{{Sfn|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 *: この木枯神社の旧鎮座地の社叢は「木枯杜（こがらしのもり、木枯森）」と称されたという。「木枯森」は、『{{jalink|枕草子|Q615137}}』第194段の「森は」で取り上げられるほか、『{{jalink|後撰和歌集|Q1538538}}』などで{{jalink|歌枕|Q1588579}}としても見えるが、それらをこの山城の木枯神社に比定する説と、別に駿河の木枯神社（{{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|静岡市|Q174691}}）に比定する説とがある（「{{jalink|木枯森|Q60997800}}」も参照）{{Sfn|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}[http://japanknowledge.com/articles/blogjournal/interest_chimei/entry.html?entryid=112 第112回 木枯森（木枯杜）]（日本歴史地名大系ジャーナル<ジャパンナレッジ>）。。なお『能因歌枕』（平安時代中期の歌学書）では、山城国・駿河国の両方が歌枕として採用され、掲載されている{{Sfn|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 * 三神の社 - 祭神：瓊々杵命 * 八幡社 - 祭神：応神天皇 * 稲積社 - 祭神：宇迦御魂神 === 祭事 === [[File:CowFesta.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|『{{jalink|都名所図会|Q11643495}}』「太秦牛祭図絵」}}]] 大酒神社の祭礼としては、{{jalink|10月12日|Q2920}}夜（古くは{{jalink|9月12日 (旧暦)|Q840306|旧暦9月12日}}）に行われる'''牛祭'''（うしまつり）が知られる。{{jalink|長和|Q1194173}}年間（{{jalink|1012年|Q19239}}-{{jalink|1017年|Q19249}}）に{{jalink|源信 (僧侶)|Q1009263|源信}}（恵心僧都）が念仏守護神として{{jalink|摩多羅神|Q6380689|麻多羅神}}を勧請したのに始まると伝え、神仏習合期は広隆寺の僧侶によって斎行された。江戸時代の祭の様子は『{{jalink|都名所図会|Q11643495}}』にも描かれている{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}{{Sfn|大酒神社（神々）|1986年}}。 祭りでは異形の面をつけた麻多羅神役の者が牛に乗り、四天王・行列を従えて広隆寺境内を練り歩き、最後に薬師堂前で祭文を読み上げる。この牛祭は京都の三大奇祭の1つに数えられ、京都市登録無形民俗文化財に登録されている[http://www.city.kyoto.jp/bunshi/bunkazai/siteisya、sinn/muminsetumei.htm#Anchor-FZ~UFW~d-49001 京都市指定・登録文化財 -無形民俗- > 太秦牛祭]（京都市ホームページ）。{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}{{Sfn|大酒神社（神々）|1986年}}（「{{jalink|広隆寺#牛祭|Q3076920}}」も参照）。 === 文化財 === === 京都市登録文化財 === * 無形民俗文化財 ** 太秦牛祭 - 保護団体は牛祭保存会。1983年（昭和58年）6月1日登録[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005877.html 京都市指定・登録文化財 -無形民俗文化財- 右京区]（京都市ホームページ）。。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''原典''' {{reflist|group="原"}} '''出典''' {{reflist|2}} === 参考文献 === * 境内説明板 * {{Cite book|和書|editor=式内社研究会|author=笠井倭人|chapter=大酒神社|year=1979|title=式内社調査報告 第1巻|publisher={{jalink|皇學館大学|Q1797584}}出版部|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=大酒神社|title={{jalink|日本歴史地名大系|Q17213509}} 27 京都市の地名|publisher={{jalink|平凡社|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582910122|ref={{Harvid|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}}} ** {{Wikicite|reference=「大酒神社」|ref={{Harvid|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}}}、{{Wikicite|reference=「木枯神社」|ref={{Harvid|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}}}。 * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1982|chapter=大酒神社|title={{jalink|角川日本地名大辞典|Q4093011}} 26-1 京都府|publisher={{jalink|角川書店|Q1054765}}|isbn=4040012615|ref={{Harvid|大酒神社（角川）|1982年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=谷川健一|editor-link=谷川健一|author=大和岩雄|authorlink=大和岩雄|year=1986|chapter=大酒神社|title=日本の神々 -神社と聖地- 5 山城・近江|publisher={{jalink|白水社|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022151|ref={{Harvid|大酒神社（神々）|1986年}}}} === 関連項目 === * {{jalink|広隆寺|Q3076920}} * {{jalink|大避神社|Q11439397}}（兵庫県赤穂市） {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおさけしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Osake Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Osake Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Osake Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Q3029647|lt=Q3029647|WD=Q3029647}} == P14005 (P14005) == * {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|ko|종5위|zh|從五位|WD=Q11071125}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|lt=Q17|WD=Q17}} == Commons category (P373) == * Osake-jinja (Kyoto) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '京都府京都市右京区太秦蜂岡町', 'language': 'ja'} == Japan Search name ID (P6698) == * 大酒神社 {{commons category|Category:Osake-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[JA:大酒神社]] [[WUU:大酒神社]] {{wikidata link|Q21019665}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 大酒神社 |画像 = [[File:Osake-jinja (Kyoto) honden.JPG|270px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|京都府|Q120730}}{{jalink|京都市|Q34600}}{{jalink|右京区|Q580186}}太秦蜂岡町30 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|秦|Q34756}}{{jalink|始皇帝|Q7192}}
{{jalink|弓月君|Q11066250|弓月王}}
{{jalink|秦酒公|Q54867522}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}} |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = {{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = |神事 = {{jalink|#祭事||牛祭}}（{{jalink|10月12日|Q2920}}） |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto city|label=大酒神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} [[File:Osake-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|鳥居}}]] '''大酒神社'''（おおさけじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|京都府|Q120730}}{{jalink|京都市|Q34600}}{{jalink|右京区|Q580186}}太秦蜂岡町にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}。 === 祭神 === 祭神は次の5柱境内説明板。。 ; 主祭神 :* {{jalink|秦|Q34756}}{{jalink|始皇帝|Q7192}} :* {{jalink|弓月君|Q11066250|弓月王}}（ゆずきのきみ） :* {{jalink|秦酒公|Q54867522}}（はたのさけのきみ） ; 相殿神 :* 兄媛命（えひめのみこと） - 呉服女。 :* 弟媛命（おとひめのみこと） - 漢織女。 『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}での祭神の記載は1座。同帳では「大酒神社{{sub| 元名大辟神}}」とあることから、元の神名は「'''大辟神'''」であったことが知られる{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 大酒神について、{{jalink|広隆寺|Q3076920}}の{{jalink|縁起|Q522046}}である『広隆寺来由記』（山城州葛野郡楓野大堰郷広隆寺来由記、{{jalink|明応|Q1193841}}8年（{{jalink|1499年|Q6708}}）成立）[{{NDLDC|1879799/384}} 『群書類従 新校 第十八巻』]（内外書籍、1931年-1937年、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）384-388コマ。では{{jalink|秦|Q34756}}の{{jalink|始皇帝|Q7192}}の祖神とし、日本に渡来した{{jalink|功満王}}（秦{{jalink|始皇帝|Q7192}}の後裔、{{jalink|秦氏|Q987290}}遠祖）が{{jalink|勧請}}したとする{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。伝承自体の真偽は明らかでないが、伝承に見えるように古くから太秦一帯を開発した秦氏の氏神であったと推測され{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}、史書に見える秦酒公（はたのさけのきみ）と大酒神を関連づける説もある。一方{{jalink|近世|Q5308718}}の『{{jalink|雍州府志|Q18460389}}』では祭神を{{jalink|物部守屋|Q2424736}}とし、一説に{{jalink|秦河勝|Q5680877}}とする{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。また『{{jalink|都名所図会|Q11643495}}』では祭神を{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602}}・{{jalink|八幡神|Q261637}}・天満天神とし、別説に秦の始皇帝とする説や、秦河勝とする説を載せる{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 これらとは別に近年の研究では、元の神名の「大辟（大避）」から、「災厄を避ける（遠ざける）」いわゆる{{jalink|道祖神|Q269515}}とする説が挙げられている{{Sfn|大酒神社（神々）|1986年}}。この説では、『空華日工集』・『広隆寺縁起』において{{jalink|神体|Q327532}}が石と記されることと、一般に石神の多くが{{jalink|サルタヒコ|Q3090037|猿田彦神}}（道祖神）とされることとが関連づけて指摘される{{Sfn|大酒神社（神々）|1986年}}。一方、「オホサケ」を「大裂」の意とし、土木技術によって大地・川を裂き開拓を行なった秦氏をたたえる神格とする説もある{{jalink|水谷千秋|Q11549157}} 『謎の渡来人秦氏（文春新書734）』 文藝春秋、2009年、pp. 55-59。{{jalink|菅澤庸子|Q45819933}} {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/documents/1296808791808.pdf 「広隆寺」]}}『[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/yukari06.html 人権 ゆかりの地をたずねて 京都市内編]』 京都人権啓発推進会議、2001年、pp. 26-27（リンクは京都府ホームページ）。。 このように古代の祭神の神格は不詳ながら、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}16年（{{jalink|1883年|Q7816}}）の神社明細帳では上記5柱が祭神として記され、現在に継承されている{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 === 歴史 === === 創建 === [[File:Kouryuji Roumon.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|{{jalink|広隆寺|Q3076920}}}}]] 創建に関して『広隆寺来由記』では、{{jalink|仲哀天皇|Q179971}}（第14代）の時に渡来した{{jalink|功満王}}（秦始皇帝の後裔、弓月君の父親）が「秦始皇之祖神」を{{jalink|勧請}}したことに始まるとする{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 現在は{{jalink|広隆寺|Q3076920}}の東隣に小祠として鎮座するが、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}の{{jalink|神仏分離|Q1970535}}以前には広隆寺の桂宮院内に鎮座する{{jalink|伽藍神|Q10885799}}であった{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 === 概史 === {{jalink|六国史|Q1151168|国史}}では、{{jalink|嘉祥|Q1373157}}2年（{{jalink|849年|Q23902}}）に{{jalink|山城国|Q749276}}{{jalink|葛野郡|Q13579879}}の「大辟神」が霊験のある社であるとして従五位下の{{jalink|神階|Q11591025}}に叙せられた旨が記されている{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 {{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}では山城国葛野郡に「大酒神社 小 元名大辟神」として、{{jalink|式内社}}に列するとともに元々は「大辟神」の神名である旨が記載されている{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 『広隆寺来由記』では、「大酒大明神」の神階が順次昇叙され、{{jalink|治暦|Q934365}}4年（{{jalink|1068年|Q19421}}）には正一位の極位に達した旨が記されている{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。また同記では、{{jalink|延喜|Q1374325}}年間（{{jalink|901年|Q23580}}-{{jalink|923年|Q30108}}）に広隆寺に{{jalink|勧請}}された鎮守38所の1所として「大酒{{sub| 正一位}}」と見える{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 {{jalink|南北朝時代 (日本)|Q264012|南北朝時代}}の『空華日工集』以降、文献では広隆寺桂宮院の鎮守社として見え、広隆寺とともに推移した{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 {{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}後、{{jalink|神仏分離|Q1970535}}によって現在地に移転し{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534}}では{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}に列した{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 === 神階 === * 六国史時代における{{jalink|神階|Q11591025}}奉叙の記録 ** {{jalink|嘉祥|Q1373157}}2年（{{jalink|849年|Q23902}}）9月16日、従五位下 （『{{jalink|続日本後紀|Q1041301}}』）『続日本後紀』嘉祥2年（849年）9月丙寅（16日）条。 - 表記は「大辟神」。 * 六国史以後 ** {{jalink|朱雀天皇|Q361019}}年間（{{jalink|930年|Q23409}}-{{jalink|946年|Q23893}}）、従三位 （『広隆寺来由記』） ** {{jalink|天慶|Q1327340}}4年（{{jalink|941年|Q24255}}）5月15日、正三位 （『広隆寺来由記』） ** {{jalink|天喜|Q1193591}}3年（{{jalink|1055年|Q19371}}）11月20日、正二位 （『広隆寺来由記』） ** {{jalink|治暦|Q934365}}4年（{{jalink|1068年|Q19421}}）4月25日、正一位 （『広隆寺来由記』） === 摂末社 === 『式内社調査報告』では次の4社が境内社として記載されている{{Sfn|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}。 * 木枯神社 *: 祭神は大国主命。『{{jalink|日本三代実録|Q1052981}}』{{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|貞観}}4年（{{jalink|862年|Q24993}}）条『日本三代実録』貞観4年（862年）4月24日条。の「木枯神」に比定される{{jalink|国史見在社|Q118304363}}（異説あり）「木枯神」の比定地には、別に{{jalink|藤原順子|Q11623640}}（{{jalink|仁明天皇|Q349271}}女御）の東五条院（現・京都市下京区）内とする説がある {{Harv|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。{{Sfn|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。『広隆寺来由記』では「木枯明神」と見え、{{jalink|薬師如来|Q854773}}像を広隆寺に迎えた際、向日明神が広隆寺門前の槻の木を枯らしたが、この神を「木枯明神」として別に祀ると槻の木は蘇ったという{{Sfn|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。現在の大酒神社境内への遷座時期は詳らかでないが、『山城名勝志』でも広隆寺の大門南の民家後園に鎮座すると見えることから、それ以降のことと推測される{{Sfn|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 *: この木枯神社の旧鎮座地の社叢は「木枯杜（こがらしのもり、木枯森）」と称されたという。「木枯森」は、『{{jalink|枕草子|Q615137}}』第194段の「森は」で取り上げられるほか、『{{jalink|後撰和歌集|Q1538538}}』などで{{jalink|歌枕|Q1588579}}としても見えるが、それらをこの山城の木枯神社に比定する説と、別に駿河の木枯神社（{{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|静岡市|Q174691}}）に比定する説とがある（「{{jalink|木枯森|Q60997800}}」も参照）{{Sfn|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}[http://japanknowledge.com/articles/blogjournal/interest_chimei/entry.html?entryid=112 第112回 木枯森（木枯杜）]（日本歴史地名大系ジャーナル<ジャパンナレッジ>）。。なお『能因歌枕』（平安時代中期の歌学書）では、山城国・駿河国の両方が歌枕として採用され、掲載されている{{Sfn|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}。 * 三神の社 - 祭神：瓊々杵命 * 八幡社 - 祭神：応神天皇 * 稲積社 - 祭神：宇迦御魂神 === 祭事 === [[File:CowFesta.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|『{{jalink|都名所図会|Q11643495}}』「太秦牛祭図絵」}}]] 大酒神社の祭礼としては、{{jalink|10月12日|Q2920}}夜（古くは{{jalink|9月12日 (旧暦)|Q840306|旧暦9月12日}}）に行われる'''牛祭'''（うしまつり）が知られる。{{jalink|長和|Q1194173}}年間（{{jalink|1012年|Q19239}}-{{jalink|1017年|Q19249}}）に{{jalink|源信 (僧侶)|Q1009263|源信}}（恵心僧都）が念仏守護神として{{jalink|摩多羅神|Q6380689|麻多羅神}}を勧請したのに始まると伝え、神仏習合期は広隆寺の僧侶によって斎行された。江戸時代の祭の様子は『{{jalink|都名所図会|Q11643495}}』にも描かれている{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}{{Sfn|大酒神社（神々）|1986年}}。 祭りでは異形の面をつけた麻多羅神役の者が牛に乗り、四天王・行列を従えて広隆寺境内を練り歩き、最後に薬師堂前で祭文を読み上げる。この牛祭は京都の三大奇祭の1つに数えられ、京都市登録無形民俗文化財に登録されている[http://www.city.kyoto.jp/bunshi/bunkazai/siteisya、sinn/muminsetumei.htm#Anchor-FZ~UFW~d-49001 京都市指定・登録文化財 -無形民俗- > 太秦牛祭]（京都市ホームページ）。{{Sfn|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}{{Sfn|大酒神社（神々）|1986年}}（「{{jalink|広隆寺#牛祭|Q3076920}}」も参照）。 === 文化財 === === 京都市登録文化財 === * 無形民俗文化財 ** 太秦牛祭 - 保護団体は牛祭保存会。1983年（昭和58年）6月1日登録[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005877.html 京都市指定・登録文化財 -無形民俗文化財- 右京区]（京都市ホームページ）。。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''原典''' {{reflist|group="原"}} '''出典''' {{reflist|2}} === 参考文献 === * 境内説明板 * {{Cite book|和書|editor=式内社研究会|author=笠井倭人|chapter=大酒神社|year=1979|title=式内社調査報告 第1巻|publisher={{jalink|皇學館大学|Q1797584}}出版部|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|大酒神社（式内社）|1979年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=大酒神社|title={{jalink|日本歴史地名大系|Q17213509}} 27 京都市の地名|publisher={{jalink|平凡社|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582910122|ref={{Harvid|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}}} ** {{Wikicite|reference=「大酒神社」|ref={{Harvid|大酒神社（平凡社）|1979年}}}}、{{Wikicite|reference=「木枯神社」|ref={{Harvid|木枯神社（平凡社）|1979年}}}}。 * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1982|chapter=大酒神社|title={{jalink|角川日本地名大辞典|Q4093011}} 26-1 京都府|publisher={{jalink|角川書店|Q1054765}}|isbn=4040012615|ref={{Harvid|大酒神社（角川）|1982年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=谷川健一|editor-link=谷川健一|author=大和岩雄|authorlink=大和岩雄|year=1986|chapter=大酒神社|title=日本の神々 -神社と聖地- 5 山城・近江|publisher={{jalink|白水社|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022151|ref={{Harvid|大酒神社（神々）|1986年}}}} === 関連項目 === * {{jalink|広隆寺|Q3076920}} * {{jalink|大避神社|Q11439397}}（兵庫県赤穂市） {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおさけしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Osake Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Osake Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Osake Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] It is located in [[Yamashiro Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyoto City|Q34600}}{{jalink|Ukyo Ward|Q580186}}30 Umasa Hachiokacho |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity enshrined = {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}}{{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}}
{{jalink|Bow Moon King|Q11066250|Bow Moon King}}
{{jalink|Qin Shugong|Q54867522}} |Divinity = |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}} (small)
Old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = |Shinto Ritual = {{jalink|#Festival||Cow Festival}} ({{jalink|October 12th|Q2920}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto city|label=Taizake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} [[File:Osake-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Osake Shrine'' (Osake Jinja) is located in {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyoto City|Q34600}}{{jalink|Ukyo Ward|Q580186}}Umasa Hachioka-cho.{{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}}. In {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}}. === Enshrined God === The following five deities are enshrined. . ; Main enshrined deity :* {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}}{{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}} :* {{jalink|Yuzuki-kun|Q11066250|Yuzuki-kun}} (Yuzuki no Kimi) :* {{jalink|Qin Shugong|Q54867522}} (Hatanosake no Kimi) ; Aidenjin :* Ehime no Mikoto - Kimono woman. :* Otohime no Mikoto - Han weaver. In "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}, there is one enshrined deity. In the same book, it is written as ``Osake Shrine {{sub| former name Daisojin}}, so it is known that the original name of the god was ``''Daisojin'''' {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine) | 1979}}.About the god of sake, "Koryuji Raiyuki" (Yamashiro Shukatsu Nogori Kaedeno Oigo-go Koryuji temple history, established in {{jalink|Meio|Q1193841}}8 ({{jalink|1499|Q6708}})[{{NDLDC|1879799/384}} "Gunshoju Shinkyo Volume 18"] (Naigai Publishing, 1931-1937, National Diet Library Digital Collection) 384-388 frames. is said to be the ancestral god of {{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}} of {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}}, who came to Japan. It is said that a descendant of the Qin Shihuang (Q7192), a distant ancestor of the Qin clan (Q987290), proposed this shrine in 1979. The authenticity of the legend itself is not clear, but as the legend suggests, it is presumed to have been the guardian deity of the Hata clan, which developed the Uzumasa area from ancient times {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}}, and there is also a theory that links the god of sake with Hatanosake no Kimi, who appears in history books. On the other hand, in {{jalink|Early modern|Q5308718}}'s ``{{jalink|Yongshufushi|Q18460389}}'', the enshrined deity was {{jalink|Monobe Moriya|Q2424736}}, and one theory says that it was {{jalink|Hata Kawakatsu|Q5680877}}{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. Also, in "{{jalink|Capital Meisho Zue|Q11643495}}", the enshrined deities are {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|eight Hatakami|Q261637}} Tenma Tenjin, and there are other theories that say it is Qin Shihuang and others that say it is Hata Kawakatsu {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}.In addition to these, recent research has suggested that the original name of the god, ``大辟 (大辟),'' means the so-called {{jalink|Dōsojin|Q269515}}, which ``avoids (keeps away) disasters.''{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. In this theory, it is pointed out that {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}} is written as stone in "Kuka Nikkoshu" and "Koryuji Engi" and that many stone deities are generally said to be {{jalink|Sartahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahikokami}} (Dosojin) {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. On the other hand, there is also a theory that ``Ohosake'' means ``large rift,'' and that it is a deity that honors the Hata clan, who split the land and rivers with their civil engineering skills and cultivated land. 55-59. {{jalink|Yoko Sugazawa|Q45819933}} {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/documents/1296808791808.pdf "Koryuji"]}}'[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/yukari06.html Human Rights Inquiring about places of connection to Kyoto City] Kyoto Human Rights Awareness Promotion Council, 2001, pp. 26-27 (link to Kyoto Prefecture homepage). .Although the deity of the ancient enshrined deities is unknown, the above-mentioned five pillars were listed as enshrined deities in the shrine statement book of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}16 ({{jalink|1883|Q7816}}), and they have been inherited to the present day. === History === === Founding === [[File:Kouryuji Roumon.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|{{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}}}}]] Regarding its founding, "Koryu-ji Raiyuki" states that it was founded by the {{jalink|King Man}} (King Mangō}} (first generation of It is said that it began when a descendant of the emperor, Yuzuki-kun's father) prayed to the ``God of the ancestors of the First Qin Emperor.'' Currently, it is enshrined as a small shrine next to the east of {{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}}, but {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism| Q1970535}} Previously, it was a {{jalink|Garanjin|Q10885799}} enshrined within the Katsuramiya-in of Koryu-ji Temple. === Outline === In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}, {{jalink|Kaxiang|Q1373157}}2 years ({{jalink|849 years|Q23902}}) , the ``Daisojin'' of {{jalink|Yamashiro Country|Q749276}}{{jalink|Kazuno District|Q13579879}} has a miraculous experience. It is recorded that it was conferred the rank of {{jalink|Divine Grade|Q11591025}}, which is lower than the Jugoi (Junior Fifth Rank) as a shrine {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. {{jalink|Extension (era name)|Q1327274|Extension}} 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" It is listed in {{jalink|Shikiuchi-sha}} as the original name of ``Osake-jinja Shrine (Shikiuchi-jinja)'', and it is also stated that it was originally the divine name of ``Osake-jinja Shrine (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}}.In "Koryuji Raiyuki" , it is written that the shrine of ``Osake Daimyojin'' was promoted one after another, and in {{jalink|Jireki|Q934365}}4 year ({{jalink|1068|Q19421}}) it reached the highest rank of Shoichii {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1979}}. Also, in the same article, it is said that it is one of the 38 shrines that were given to Koryuji during the {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}} years ({{jalink|901|Q23580}}-{{jalink|923|Q30108}}) as one of the 38 shrines that were designated as shrines to Koryuji. {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}} After the "Kuka Nikkoshu" in the Nanbokucho period}}, it appears in literature as the guardian shrine of Katsuragu-in of Koryu-ji Temple, and it moved along with Koryu-ji Temple {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, it was moved to its current location due to {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}. }, {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}} was ranked as {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}} in {{jalink|Modern shrine rank system|Q712534}}. === God level === * Record of {{jalink|Divine rank|Q11591025}} dedication during the Six Kingdoms historical period ** September 16, 2nd year of {{jalink|Kasho|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|849|Q23902}}), Junior Fifth Rank ("{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Koki|Q1041301}}") "Shoku Nihon Koki" Article written on September 16th, Kasho 2nd year (849). - The notation is ``Great God''. * After Six Kingdoms History ** {{jalink|Emperor Suzaku|Q361019}} years ({{jalink|930|Q23409}}-{{jalink|946|Q23893}}), Jusanmi (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** May 15, 4th year of {{jalink|Tenkei|Q1327340}} ({{jalink|941|Q24255}}), Shosanmi (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** November 20, {{jalink|Tenki|Q1193591}}3 years ({{jalink|1055 years|Q19371}}), Shonin 2 (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** April 25, {{jalink|Jireki|Q934365}}4th year ({{jalink|1068|Q19421}}), Shoichii (Koryuji Raiyuki) === Setsusha === In the "Shikiuchi-sha Investigation Report", the following four shrines are listed as shrines within the precincts. * Kogare Shrine *: The enshrined deity is Okuninushi no Mikoto. "{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsoroku|Q1052981}}" {{jalink|Jokan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōkan}} 4th year ({{jalink|862|Q24993}}) Article {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. “Koryuji Temple Raiyuki” , it appears as ``Kokare Myojin,'' and it is said that when the statue of {{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}} was brought to Koryuji Temple, Muko Myojin withered the Japanese japanese tree in front of the gate of Koryuji Temple, but when this deity was enshrined separately as ``Kokare Myojin,'' the tree was revived.{{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. It is not clear when it was moved to the current precincts of Taizaka Shrine, but it is presumed to have been moved after that time, as it appears in "Yamashiro Meishoshi" that it was enshrined in the rear garden of a private house south of the main gate of Koryuji Temple {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}.*: It is said that the shrine complex at the former enshrinement site of Kogarashi-jinja Shrine was called ``Kogara-no-mori'' (Kogara-no-mori). ``Kokare Mori'' is mentioned in ``Moriwa'' in the 194th paragraph of ``{{jalink|Makura Book|Q615137}}'' and is also seen as ``{{jalink|utamakura|Q1588579}}'' in ``{{jalink|later selected Japanese poetry collection|Q1538538}}'', but these are compared to the Kogare Shrine in Yamashiro. There is a theory that compares it to Kokare Shrine in Suruga ({{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}}) (see also "{{jalink|Kokare Mori|Q60997800}}") {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}[http://japanknowledge.com/articles/blogjournal/interest_chimei/entry.html?entryid=112 112th Kogare Mori (Kokare Mori)] (Japanese historical place name journal ). . In addition, in "Noin Utamakura" (a poetry book from the middle of the Heian period), both Yamashiro Province and Suruga Province are adopted and listed as Utamakura. * Shrine of the Three Gods - Enshrined Deity: Keirigi no Mikoto * Hachiman Shrine - Enshrined deity: Emperor Ojin * Inazumi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Ukamitama no kami === Festival === [[File:CowFesta.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|"{{jalink|Tokyo Famous Places|Q11643495}}" "Uzumasa Cow Festival Pictures"}}]] One of the festivals at Osaka Shrine is known as the ``Ushimatsuri,'' which is held on the night of {{jalink|October 12th|Q2920}} (previously known as {{jalink|September 12th (old calendar)|Q840306|September 12th of the lunar calendar}}). {{jalink|Nagawa|Q1194173}} ({{jalink|1012|Q19239}}-{{jalink|1017|Q19249}}) (Monk)|Q1009263|Genshin}} (Eshin Sozu) is said to have started praying for {{jalink|Matarajin|Q6380689|Matarajin}} as the guardian deity of Nembutsu, and during the period of syncretism of Shintoism and Buddhism, it was performed by the monks of Koryuji Temple. The Edo period festival is also depicted in ``{{jalink|Capital Meisho Zue|Q11643495}}''.During the festival, a person wearing a strange mask who plays the role of Matara-jin rides a bull and parades around the grounds of Koryu-ji Temple accompanied by the Four Heavenly Kings and a procession, and finally reads out the ritual in front of Yakushi-do Hall. This cow festival is counted as one of Kyoto's three strange festivals and is registered as a Kyoto City Registered Intangible Folk Cultural Property. Uzumasa Beef Festival] (Kyoto City homepage). {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}} (Also see "{{jalink|Koryuji #Cow Festival|Q3076920}}"). === Cultural assets === === Kyoto City registered cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Uzumasa Cow Festival - The preservation organization is the Cow Festival Preservation Society. Registered on June 1, 1983 [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005877.html Kyoto City designated/registered cultural property - Intangible folk cultural property - Ukyo Ward] (Kyoto City homepage). . === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Precinct information University|Q1797584}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=Osake Shrine|title={{jalink|Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509}} 27 Kyoto city place name|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582910122|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Osake Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Kikare Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kikare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}}. * {{Cite book|Japanese Old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = |Shinto Ritual = {{jalink|#Festival||Cow Festival}} ({{jalink|October 12th|Q2920}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto city|label=Taizake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} [[File:Osake-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Osake Shrine'' (Osake Jinja) is located in {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyoto City|Q34600}}{{jalink|Ukyo Ward|Q580186}}Umasa Hachioka-cho.{{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}}. In {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}}. === Enshrined God === The following five deities are enshrined. . ; Main enshrined deity :* {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}}{{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}} :* {{jalink|Yuzuki-kun|Q11066250|Yuzuki-kun}} (Yuzuki no Kimi) :* {{jalink|Qin Shugong|Q54867522}} (Hatanosake no Kimi) ; Aidenjin :* Ehime no Mikoto - Kimono woman. :* Otohime no Mikoto - Han weaver. In "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}, there is one enshrined deity. In the same book, it is written as ``Osake Shrine {{sub| former name Daisojin}}, so it is known that the original name of the god was ``''Daisojin'''' {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine) | 1979}}.About the god of sake, "Koryuji Raiyuki" (Yamashiro Shukatsu Nogori Kaedeno Oigo-go Koryuji temple history, established in {{jalink|Meio|Q1193841}}8 ({{jalink|1499|Q6708}})[{{NDLDC|1879799/384}} "Gunshoju Shinkyo Volume 18"] (Naigai Publishing, 1931-1937, National Diet Library Digital Collection) 384-388 frames. is said to be the ancestral god of {{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}} of {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}}, who came to Japan. It is said that a descendant of the Qin Shihuang (Q7192), a distant ancestor of the Qin clan (Q987290), proposed this shrine in 1979. The authenticity of the legend itself is not clear, but as the legend suggests, it is presumed to have been the guardian deity of the Hata clan, which developed the Uzumasa area from ancient times {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}}, and there is also a theory that links the god of sake with Hatanosake no Kimi, who appears in history books. On the other hand, in {{jalink|Early modern|Q5308718}}'s ``{{jalink|Yongshufushi|Q18460389}}'', the enshrined deity was {{jalink|Monobe Moriya|Q2424736}}, and one theory says that it was {{jalink|Hata Kawakatsu|Q5680877}}{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. Also, in "{{jalink|Capital Meisho Zue|Q11643495}}", the enshrined deities are {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|eight Hatakami|Q261637}} Tenma Tenjin, and there are other theories that say it is Qin Shihuang and others that say it is Hata Kawakatsu {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}.In addition to these, recent research has suggested that the original name of the god, ``大辟 (大辟),'' means the so-called {{jalink|Dōsojin|Q269515}}, which ``avoids (keeps away) disasters.''{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. In this theory, it is pointed out that {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}} is written as stone in "Kuka Nikkoshu" and "Koryuji Engi" and that many stone deities are generally said to be {{jalink|Sartahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahikokami}} (Dosojin) {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. On the other hand, there is also a theory that ``Ohosake'' means ``large rift,'' and that it is a deity that honors the Hata clan, who split the land and rivers with their civil engineering skills and cultivated land. 55-59. {{jalink|Yoko Sugazawa|Q45819933}} {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/documents/1296808791808.pdf "Koryuji"]}}'[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/yukari06.html Human Rights Inquiring about places of connection to Kyoto City] Kyoto Human Rights Awareness Promotion Council, 2001, pp. 26-27 (link to Kyoto Prefecture homepage). .Although the deity of the ancient enshrined deities is unknown, the above-mentioned five pillars were listed as enshrined deities in the shrine statement book of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}16 ({{jalink|1883|Q7816}}), and they have been inherited to the present day. === History === === Founding === [[File:Kouryuji Roumon.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|{{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}}}}]] Regarding its founding, "Koryu-ji Raiyuki" states that it was founded by the {{jalink|King Man}} (King Mangō}} (first generation of It is said that it began when a descendant of the emperor, Yuzuki-kun's father) prayed to the ``God of the ancestors of the First Qin Emperor.'' Currently, it is enshrined as a small shrine next to the east of {{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}}, but {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism| Q1970535}} Previously, it was a {{jalink|Garanjin|Q10885799}} enshrined within the Katsuramiya-in of Koryu-ji Temple. === Outline === In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}, {{jalink|Kaxiang|Q1373157}}2 years ({{jalink|849 years|Q23902}}) , the ``Daisojin'' of {{jalink|Yamashiro Country|Q749276}}{{jalink|Kazuno District|Q13579879}} has a miraculous experience. It is recorded that it was conferred the rank of {{jalink|Divine Grade|Q11591025}}, which is lower than the Jugoi (Junior Fifth Rank) as a shrine {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. {{jalink|Extension (era name)|Q1327274|Extension}} 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" It is listed in {{jalink|Shikiuchi-sha}} as the original name of ``Osake-jinja Shrine (Shikiuchi-jinja)'', and it is also stated that it was originally the divine name of ``Osake-jinja Shrine (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}}.In "Koryuji Raiyuki" , it is written that the shrine of ``Osake Daimyojin'' was promoted one after another, and in {{jalink|Jireki|Q934365}}4 year ({{jalink|1068|Q19421}}) it reached the highest rank of Shoichii {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1979}}. Also, in the same article, it is said that it is one of the 38 shrines that were given to Koryuji during the {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}} years ({{jalink|901|Q23580}}-{{jalink|923|Q30108}}) as one of the 38 shrines that were designated as shrines to Koryuji. {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}} After the "Kuka Nikkoshu" in the Nanbokucho period}}, it appears in literature as the guardian shrine of Katsuragu-in of Koryu-ji Temple, and it moved along with Koryu-ji Temple {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, it was moved to its current location due to {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}. }, {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}} was ranked as {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}} in {{jalink|Modern shrine rank system|Q712534}}. === God level === * Record of {{jalink|Divine rank|Q11591025}} dedication during the Six Kingdoms historical period ** September 16, 2nd year of {{jalink|Kasho|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|849|Q23902}}), Junior Fifth Rank ("{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Koki|Q1041301}}") "Shoku Nihon Koki" Article written on September 16th, Kasho 2nd year (849). - The notation is ``Great God''. * After Six Kingdoms History ** {{jalink|Emperor Suzaku|Q361019}} years ({{jalink|930|Q23409}}-{{jalink|946|Q23893}}), Jusanmi (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** May 15, 4th year of {{jalink|Tenkei|Q1327340}} ({{jalink|941|Q24255}}), Shosanmi (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** November 20, {{jalink|Tenki|Q1193591}}3 years ({{jalink|1055 years|Q19371}}), Shonin 2 (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** April 25, {{jalink|Jireki|Q934365}}4th year ({{jalink|1068|Q19421}}), Shoichii (Koryuji Raiyuki) === Setsusha === In the "Shikiuchi-sha Investigation Report", the following four shrines are listed as shrines within the precincts. * Kogare Shrine *: The enshrined deity is Okuninushi no Mikoto. "{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsoroku|Q1052981}}" {{jalink|Jokan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōkan}} 4th year ({{jalink|862|Q24993}}) Article {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. “Koryuji Temple Raiyuki” , it appears as ``Kokare Myojin,'' and it is said that when the statue of {{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}} was brought to Koryuji Temple, Muko Myojin withered the Japanese japanese tree in front of the gate of Koryuji Temple, but when this deity was enshrined separately as ``Kokare Myojin,'' the tree was revived.{{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. It is not clear when it was moved to the current precincts of Taizaka Shrine, but it is presumed to have been moved after that time, as it appears in "Yamashiro Meishoshi" that it was enshrined in the rear garden of a private house south of the main gate of Koryuji Temple {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}.*: It is said that the shrine complex at the former enshrinement site of Kogarashi-jinja Shrine was called ``Kogara-no-mori'' (Kogara-no-mori). ``Kokare Mori'' is mentioned in ``Moriwa'' in the 194th paragraph of ``{{jalink|Makura Book|Q615137}}'' and is also seen as ``{{jalink|utamakura|Q1588579}}'' in ``{{jalink|later selected Japanese poetry collection|Q1538538}}'', but these are compared to the Kogare Shrine in Yamashiro. There is a theory that compares it to Kokare Shrine in Suruga ({{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}}) (see also "{{jalink|Kokare Mori|Q60997800}}") {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}[http://japanknowledge.com/articles/blogjournal/interest_chimei/entry.html?entryid=112 112th Kogare Mori (Kokare Mori)] (Japanese historical place name journal ). . In addition, in "Noin Utamakura" (a poetry book from the middle of the Heian period), both Yamashiro Province and Suruga Province are adopted and listed as Utamakura. * Shrine of the Three Gods - Enshrined Deity: Keirigi no Mikoto * Hachiman Shrine - Enshrined deity: Emperor Ojin * Inazumi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Ukamitama no kami === Festival === [[File:CowFesta.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|"{{jalink|Tokyo Famous Places|Q11643495}}" "Uzumasa Cow Festival Pictures"}}]] One of the festivals at Osaka Shrine is known as the ``Ushimatsuri,'' which is held on the night of {{jalink|October 12th|Q2920}} (previously known as {{jalink|September 12th (old calendar)|Q840306|September 12th of the lunar calendar}}). {{jalink|Nagawa|Q1194173}} ({{jalink|1012|Q19239}}-{{jalink|1017|Q19249}}) (Monk)|Q1009263|Genshin}} (Eshin Sozu) is said to have started praying for {{jalink|Matarajin|Q6380689|Matarajin}} as the guardian deity of Nembutsu, and during the period of syncretism of Shintoism and Buddhism, it was performed by the monks of Koryuji Temple. The Edo period festival is also depicted in ``{{jalink|Capital Meisho Zue|Q11643495}}''.During the festival, a person wearing a strange mask who plays the role of Matara-jin rides a bull and parades around the grounds of Koryu-ji Temple accompanied by the Four Heavenly Kings and a procession, and finally reads out the ritual in front of Yakushi-do Hall. This cow festival is counted as one of Kyoto's three strange festivals and is registered as a Kyoto City Registered Intangible Folk Cultural Property. Uzumasa Beef Festival] (Kyoto City homepage). {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}} (Also see "{{jalink|Koryuji #Cow Festival|Q3076920}}"). === Cultural assets === === Kyoto City registered cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Uzumasa Cow Festival - The preservation organization is the Cow Festival Preservation Society. Registered on June 1, 1983 [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005877.html Kyoto City designated/registered cultural property - Intangible folk cultural property - Ukyo Ward] (Kyoto City homepage). . === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Precinct information University|Q1797584}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=Osake Shrine|title={{jalink|Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509}} 27 Kyoto city place name|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582910122|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Osake Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Kikare Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kikare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}}. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1982|chapter=Osake Shrine|title={{jalink|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011}} 26-1 Kyoto Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765}}|isbn=4040012615|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Kadokawa)|1982}}}}* {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanigawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanigawa|author=Iwao Yamato|authorlink=Iwao Yamato|year=1986|chapter=Taizake Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 5 Yamashiro/Omi|publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022151|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}}} === Related items === * {{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}} * {{jalink|Osake Shrine|Q11439397}} (Ako City, Hyogo Prefecture) {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Osake Shinsiya}} == Categories The authenticity of the legend itself is not clear, but as the legend suggests, it is presumed to have been the guardian deity of the Hata clan, which developed the Uzumasa area from ancient times {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}}, and there is also a theory that links the god of sake with Hatanosake no Kimi, who appears in history books. On the other hand, in {{jalink|Early modern|Q5308718}}'s ``{{jalink|Yongshufushi|Q18460389}}'', the enshrined deity was {{jalink|Monobe Moriya|Q2424736}}, and one theory says that it was {{jalink|Hata Kawakatsu|Q5680877}}{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. Also, in "{{jalink|Capital Meisho Zue|Q11643495}}", the enshrined deities are {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|eight Hatakami|Q261637}} Tenma Tenjin, and there are other theories that say it is Qin Shihuang and others that say it is Hata Kawakatsu {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}.In addition to these, recent research has suggested that the original name of the god, ``大辟 (大辟),'' means the so-called {{jalink|Dōsojin|Q269515}}, which ``avoids (keeps away) disasters.''{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. In this theory, it is pointed out that {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}} is written as stone in "Kuka Nikkoshu" and "Koryuji Engi" and that many stone deities are generally said to be {{jalink|Sartahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahikokami}} (Dosojin) {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. On the other hand, there is also a theory that ``Ohosake'' means ``large rift,'' and that it is a deity that honors the Hata clan, who split the land and rivers with their civil engineering skills and cultivated land. 55-59. {{jalink|Yoko Sugazawa|Q45819933}} {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/documents/1296808791808.pdf "Koryuji"]}}'[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/yukari06.html Human Rights Inquiring about places of connection to Kyoto City] Kyoto Human Rights Awareness Promotion Council, 2001, pp. 26-27 (link to Kyoto Prefecture homepage). .Although the deity of the ancient enshrined deities is unknown, the above-mentioned five pillars were listed as enshrined deities in the shrine statement book of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}16 ({{jalink|1883|Q7816}}), and they have been inherited to the present day. === History === === Founding === [[File:Kouryuji Roumon.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|{{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}}}}]] Regarding its founding, "Koryu-ji Raiyuki" states that it was founded by the {{jalink|King Man}} (King Mangō}} (first generation of It is said that it began when a descendant of the emperor, Yuzuki-kun's father) prayed to the ``God of the ancestors of the First Qin Emperor.'' Currently, it is enshrined as a small shrine next to the east of {{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}}, but {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism| Q1970535}} Previously, it was a {{jalink|Garanjin|Q10885799}} enshrined within the Katsuramiya-in of Koryu-ji Temple. === Outline === In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}, {{jalink|Kaxiang|Q1373157}}2 years ({{jalink|849 years|Q23902}}) , the ``Daisojin'' of {{jalink|Yamashiro Country|Q749276}}{{jalink|Kazuno District|Q13579879}} has a miraculous experience. It is recorded that it was conferred the rank of {{jalink|Divine Grade|Q11591025}}, which is lower than the Jugoi (Junior Fifth Rank) as a shrine {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. {{jalink|Extension (era name)|Q1327274|Extension}} 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" It is listed in {{jalink|Shikiuchi-sha}} as the original name of ``Osake-jinja Shrine (Shikiuchi-jinja)'', and it is also stated that it was originally the divine name of ``Osake-jinja Shrine (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}}.In "Koryuji Raiyuki" , it is written that the shrine of ``Osake Daimyojin'' was promoted one after another, and in {{jalink|Jireki|Q934365}}4 year ({{jalink|1068|Q19421}}) it reached the highest rank of Shoichii {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1979}}. Also, in the same article, it is said that it is one of the 38 shrines that were given to Koryuji during the {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}} years ({{jalink|901|Q23580}}-{{jalink|923|Q30108}}) as one of the 38 shrines that were designated as shrines to Koryuji. {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}} After the "Kuka Nikkoshu" in the Nanbokucho period}}, it appears in literature as the guardian shrine of Katsuragu-in of Koryu-ji Temple, and it moved along with Koryu-ji Temple {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, it was moved to its current location due to {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}. }, {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}} was ranked as {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}} in {{jalink|Modern shrine rank system|Q712534}}. === God level === * Record of {{jalink|Divine rank|Q11591025}} dedication during the Six Kingdoms historical period ** September 16, 2nd year of {{jalink|Kasho|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|849|Q23902}}), Junior Fifth Rank ("{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Koki|Q1041301}}") "Shoku Nihon Koki" Article written on September 16th, Kasho 2nd year (849). - The notation is ``Great God''. * After Six Kingdoms History ** {{jalink|Emperor Suzaku|Q361019}} years ({{jalink|930|Q23409}}-{{jalink|946|Q23893}}), Jusanmi (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** May 15, 4th year of {{jalink|Tenkei|Q1327340}} ({{jalink|941|Q24255}}), Shosanmi (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** November 20, {{jalink|Tenki|Q1193591}}3 years ({{jalink|1055 years|Q19371}}), Shonin 2 (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** April 25, {{jalink|Jireki|Q934365}}4th year ({{jalink|1068|Q19421}}), Shoichii (Koryuji Raiyuki) === Setsusha === In the "Shikiuchi-sha Investigation Report", the following four shrines are listed as shrines within the precincts. * Kogare Shrine *: The enshrined deity is Okuninushi no Mikoto. "{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsoroku|Q1052981}}" {{jalink|Jokan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōkan}} 4th year ({{jalink|862|Q24993}}) Article {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. “Koryuji Temple Raiyuki” , it appears as ``Kokare Myojin,'' and it is said that when the statue of {{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}} was brought to Koryuji Temple, Muko Myojin withered the Japanese japanese tree in front of the gate of Koryuji Temple, but when this deity was enshrined separately as ``Kokare Myojin,'' the tree was revived.{{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. It is not clear when it was moved to the current precincts of Taizaka Shrine, but it is presumed to have been moved after that time, as it appears in "Yamashiro Meishoshi" that it was enshrined in the rear garden of a private house south of the main gate of Koryuji Temple {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}.*: It is said that the shrine complex at the former enshrinement site of Kogarashi-jinja Shrine was called ``Kogara-no-mori'' (Kogara-no-mori). ``Kokare Mori'' is mentioned in ``Moriwa'' in the 194th paragraph of ``{{jalink|Makura Book|Q615137}}'' and is also seen as ``{{jalink|utamakura|Q1588579}}'' in ``{{jalink|later selected Japanese poetry collection|Q1538538}}'', but these are compared to the Kogare Shrine in Yamashiro. There is a theory that compares it to Kokare Shrine in Suruga ({{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}}) (see also "{{jalink|Kokare Mori|Q60997800}}") {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}[http://japanknowledge.com/articles/blogjournal/interest_chimei/entry.html?entryid=112 112th Kogare Mori (Kokare Mori)] (Japanese historical place name journal ). . In addition, in "Noin Utamakura" (a poetry book from the middle of the Heian period), both Yamashiro Province and Suruga Province are adopted and listed as Utamakura. * Shrine of the Three Gods - Enshrined Deity: Keirigi no Mikoto * Hachiman Shrine - Enshrined deity: Emperor Ojin * Inazumi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Ukamitama no kami === Festival === [[File:CowFesta.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|"{{jalink|Tokyo Famous Places|Q11643495}}" "Uzumasa Cow Festival Pictures"}}]] One of the festivals at Osaka Shrine is known as the ``Ushimatsuri,'' which is held on the night of {{jalink|October 12th|Q2920}} (previously known as {{jalink|September 12th (old calendar)|Q840306|September 12th of the lunar calendar}}). {{jalink|Nagawa|Q1194173}} ({{jalink|1012|Q19239}}-{{jalink|1017|Q19249}}) (Monk)|Q1009263|Genshin}} (Eshin Sozu) is said to have started praying for {{jalink|Matarajin|Q6380689|Matarajin}} as the guardian deity of Nembutsu, and during the period of syncretism of Shintoism and Buddhism, it was performed by the monks of Koryuji Temple. The Edo period festival is also depicted in ``{{jalink|Capital Meisho Zue|Q11643495}}''.During the festival, a person wearing a strange mask who plays the role of Matara-jin rides a bull and parades around the grounds of Koryu-ji Temple accompanied by the Four Heavenly Kings and a procession, and finally reads out the ritual in front of Yakushi-do Hall. This cow festival is counted as one of Kyoto's three strange festivals and is registered as a Kyoto City Registered Intangible Folk Cultural Property. Uzumasa Beef Festival] (Kyoto City homepage). {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}} (Also see "{{jalink|Koryuji #Cow Festival|Q3076920}}"). === Cultural assets === === Kyoto City registered cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Uzumasa Cow Festival - The preservation organization is the Cow Festival Preservation Society. Registered on June 1, 1983 [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005877.html Kyoto City designated/registered cultural property - Intangible folk cultural property - Ukyo Ward] (Kyoto City homepage). . === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Shikiuchi Shrine Study Group|author=Kasai Wato|chapter=Osake Shrine|year=1979|title=Shikiuchi Shrine Research Report Volume 1|publisher={{jalink|Kogakukan University|Q1797584}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=Osake Shrine|title={{jalink|Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509}} 27 Kyoto city place name|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582910122|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Osake Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Kikare Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kikare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}}. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1982|chapter=Osake Shrine|title={{jalink|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011}} 26-1 Kyoto Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765}}|isbn=4040012615|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Kadokawa)|1982}}}}* {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanigawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanigawa|author=Iwao Yamato|authorlink=Iwao Yamato|year=1986|chapter=Taizake Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 5 Yamashiro/Omi|publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022151|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}}} === Related items === * {{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}} * {{jalink|Osake Shrine|Q11439397}} (Ako City, Hyogo Prefecture) {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Osake Shinsiya}} == Categories Old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = |Shinto Ritual = {{jalink|#Festival||Cow Festival}} ({{jalink|October 12th|Q2920}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto city|label=Taizake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} [[File:Osake-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Osake Shrine'' (Osake Jinja) is located in {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyoto City|Q34600}}{{jalink|Ukyo Ward|Q580186}}Umasa Hachioka-cho.{{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}}. In {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}}. === Enshrined God === The following five deities are enshrined. . ; Main enshrined deity :* {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}}{{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}} :* {{jalink|Yuzuki-kun|Q11066250|Yuzuki-kun}} (Yuzuki no Kimi) :* {{jalink|Qin Shugong|Q54867522}} (Hatanosake no Kimi) ; Aidenjin :* Ehime no Mikoto - Kimono woman. :* Otohime no Mikoto - Han weaver. In "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}, there is one enshrined deity. In the same book, it is written as ``Osake Shrine {{sub| former name Daisojin}}, so it is known that the original name of the god was ``''Daisojin'''' {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine) | 1979}}.About the god of sake, "Koryuji Raiyuki" (Yamashiro Shukatsu Nogori Kaedeno Oigo-go Koryuji temple history, established in {{jalink|Meio|Q1193841}}8 ({{jalink|1499|Q6708}})[{{NDLDC|1879799/384}} "Gunshoju Shinkyo Volume 18"] (Naigai Publishing, 1931-1937, National Diet Library Digital Collection) 384-388 frames. is said to be the ancestral god of {{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}} of {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}}, who came to Japan. It is said that a descendant of the Qin Shihuang (Q7192), a distant ancestor of the Qin clan (Q987290), proposed this shrine in 1979. The authenticity of the legend itself is not clear, but as the legend suggests, it is presumed to have been the guardian deity of the Hata clan, which developed the Uzumasa area from ancient times {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}}, and there is also a theory that links the god of sake with Hatanosake no Kimi, who appears in history books. On the other hand, in {{jalink|Early modern|Q5308718}}'s ``{{jalink|Yongshufushi|Q18460389}}'', the enshrined deity was {{jalink|Monobe Moriya|Q2424736}}, and one theory says that it was {{jalink|Hata Kawakatsu|Q5680877}}{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. Also, in "{{jalink|Capital Meisho Zue|Q11643495}}", the enshrined deities are {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|eight Hatakami|Q261637}} Tenma Tenjin, and there are other theories that say it is Qin Shihuang and others that say it is Hata Kawakatsu {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}.In addition to these, recent research has suggested that the original name of the god, ``大辟 (大辟),'' means the so-called {{jalink|Dōsojin|Q269515}}, which ``avoids (keeps away) disasters.''{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. In this theory, it is pointed out that {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}} is written as stone in "Kuka Nikkoshu" and "Koryuji Engi" and that many stone deities are generally said to be {{jalink|Sartahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahikokami}} (Dosojin) {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. On the other hand, there is also a theory that ``Ohosake'' means ``large rift,'' and that it is a deity that honors the Hata clan, who split the land and rivers with their civil engineering skills and cultivated land. 55-59. {{jalink|Yoko Sugazawa|Q45819933}} {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/documents/1296808791808.pdf "Koryuji"]}}'[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/yukari06.html Human Rights Inquiring about places of connection to Kyoto City] Kyoto Human Rights Awareness Promotion Council, 2001, pp. 26-27 (link to Kyoto Prefecture homepage). .Although the deity of the ancient enshrined deities is unknown, the above-mentioned five pillars were listed as enshrined deities in the shrine statement book of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}16 ({{jalink|1883|Q7816}}), and they have been inherited to the present day. === History === === Founding === [[File:Kouryuji Roumon.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|{{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}}}}]] Regarding its founding, "Koryu-ji Raiyuki" states that it was founded by the {{jalink|King Man}} (King Mangō}} (first generation of It is said that it began when a descendant of the emperor, Yuzuki-kun's father) prayed to the ``God of the ancestors of the First Qin Emperor.'' Currently, it is enshrined as a small shrine next to the east of {{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}}, but {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism| Q1970535}} Previously, it was a {{jalink|Garanjin|Q10885799}} enshrined within the Katsuramiya-in of Koryu-ji Temple. === Outline === In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}, {{jalink|Kaxiang|Q1373157}}2 years ({{jalink|849 years|Q23902}}) , the ``Daisojin'' of {{jalink|Yamashiro Country|Q749276}}{{jalink|Kazuno District|Q13579879}} has a miraculous experience. It is recorded that it was conferred the rank of {{jalink|Divine Grade|Q11591025}}, which is lower than the Jugoi (Junior Fifth Rank) as a shrine {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. {{jalink|Extension (era name)|Q1327274|Extension}} 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" It is listed in {{jalink|Shikiuchi-sha}} as the original name of ``Osake-jinja Shrine (Shikiuchi-jinja)'', and it is also stated that it was originally the divine name of ``Osake-jinja Shrine (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}}.In "Koryuji Raiyuki" , it is written that the shrine of ``Osake Daimyojin'' was promoted one after another, and in {{jalink|Jireki|Q934365}}4 year ({{jalink|1068|Q19421}}) it reached the highest rank of Shoichii {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1979}}. Also, in the same article, it is said that it is one of the 38 shrines that were given to Koryuji during the {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}} years ({{jalink|901|Q23580}}-{{jalink|923|Q30108}}) as one of the 38 shrines that were designated as shrines to Koryuji. {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}} After the "Kuka Nikkoshu" in the Nanbokucho period}}, it appears in literature as the guardian shrine of Katsuragu-in of Koryu-ji Temple, and it moved along with Koryu-ji Temple {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, it was moved to its current location due to {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}. }, {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}} was ranked as {{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}} in {{jalink|Modern shrine rank system|Q712534}}. === God level === * Record of {{jalink|Divine rank|Q11591025}} dedication during the Six Kingdoms historical period ** September 16, 2nd year of {{jalink|Kasho|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|849|Q23902}}), Junior Fifth Rank ("{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Koki|Q1041301}}") "Shoku Nihon Koki" Article written on September 16th, Kasho 2nd year (849). - The notation is ``Great God''. * After Six Kingdoms History ** {{jalink|Emperor Suzaku|Q361019}} years ({{jalink|930|Q23409}}-{{jalink|946|Q23893}}), Jusanmi (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** May 15, 4th year of {{jalink|Tenkei|Q1327340}} ({{jalink|941|Q24255}}), Shosanmi (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** November 20, {{jalink|Tenki|Q1193591}}3 years ({{jalink|1055 years|Q19371}}), Shonin 2 (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** April 25, {{jalink|Jireki|Q934365}}4th year ({{jalink|1068|Q19421}}), Shoichii (Koryuji Raiyuki) === Setsusha === In the "Shikiuchi-sha Investigation Report", the following four shrines are listed as shrines within the precincts. * Kogare Shrine *: The enshrined deity is Okuninushi no Mikoto. "{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsoroku|Q1052981}}" {{jalink|Jokan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōkan}} 4th year ({{jalink|862|Q24993}}) Article {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. “Koryuji Temple Raiyuki” , it appears as ``Kokare Myojin,'' and it is said that when the statue of {{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}} was brought to Koryuji Temple, Muko Myojin withered the Japanese japanese tree in front of the gate of Koryuji Temple, but when this deity was enshrined separately as ``Kokare Myojin,'' the tree was revived.{{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. It is not clear when it was moved to the current precincts of Taizaka Shrine, but it is presumed to have been moved after that time, as it appears in "Yamashiro Meishoshi" that it was enshrined in the rear garden of a private house south of the main gate of Koryuji Temple {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}.*: It is said that the shrine complex at the former enshrinement site of Kogarashi-jinja Shrine was called ``Kogara-no-mori'' (Kogara-no-mori). ``Kokare Mori'' is mentioned in ``Moriwa'' in the 194th paragraph of ``{{jalink|Makura Book|Q615137}}'' and is also seen as ``{{jalink|utamakura|Q1588579}}'' in ``{{jalink|later selected Japanese poetry collection|Q1538538}}'', but these are compared to the Kogare Shrine in Yamashiro. There is a theory that compares it to Kokare Shrine in Suruga ({{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}}) (see also "{{jalink|Kokare Mori|Q60997800}}") {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}[http://japanknowledge.com/articles/blogjournal/interest_chimei/entry.html?entryid=112 112th Kogare Mori (Kokare Mori)] (Japanese historical place name journal ). . In addition, in "Noin Utamakura" (a poetry book from the middle of the Heian period), both Yamashiro Province and Suruga Province are adopted and listed as Utamakura. * Shrine of the Three Gods - Enshrined Deity: Keirigi no Mikoto * Hachiman Shrine - Enshrined deity: Emperor Ojin * Inazumi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Ukamitama no kami === Festival === [[File:CowFesta.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|"{{jalink|Tokyo Famous Places|Q11643495}}" "Uzumasa Cow Festival Pictures"}}]] One of the festivals at Osaka Shrine is known as the ``Ushimatsuri,'' which is held on the night of {{jalink|October 12th|Q2920}} (previously known as {{jalink|September 12th (old calendar)|Q840306|September 12th of the lunar calendar}}). {{jalink|Nagawa|Q1194173}} ({{jalink|1012|Q19239}}-{{jalink|1017|Q19249}}) (Monk)|Q1009263|Genshin}} (Eshin Sozu) is said to have started praying for {{jalink|Matarajin|Q6380689|Matarajin}} as the guardian deity of Nembutsu, and during the period of syncretism of Shintoism and Buddhism, it was performed by the monks of Koryuji Temple. The Edo period festival is also depicted in ``{{jalink|Capital Meisho Zue|Q11643495}}''.During the festival, a person wearing a strange mask who plays the role of Matara-jin rides a bull and parades around the grounds of Koryu-ji Temple accompanied by the Four Heavenly Kings and a procession, and finally reads out the ritual in front of Yakushi-do Hall. This cow festival is counted as one of Kyoto's three strange festivals and is registered as a Kyoto City Registered Intangible Folk Cultural Property. Uzumasa Beef Festival] (Kyoto City homepage). {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}} (Also see "{{jalink|Koryuji #Cow Festival|Q3076920}}"). === Cultural assets === === Kyoto City registered cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Uzumasa Cow Festival - The preservation organization is the Cow Festival Preservation Society. Registered on June 1, 1983 [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005877.html Kyoto City designated/registered cultural property - Intangible folk cultural property - Ukyo Ward] (Kyoto City homepage). . === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Original text''' {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Shikiuchi Shrine Study Group|author=Kasai Wato|chapter=Osake Shrine|year=1979|title=Shikiuchi Shrine Research Report Volume 1|publisher={{jalink|Kogakukan University|Q1797584}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=Osake Shrine|title={{jalink|Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509}} 27 Kyoto city place name|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582910122|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Osake Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Kikare Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kikare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}}}. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1982|chapter=Osake Shrine|title={{jalink|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011}} 26-1 Kyoto Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765}}|isbn=4040012615|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Kadokawa)|1982}}}}* {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanigawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanigawa|author=Iwao Yamato|authorlink=Iwao Yamato|year=1986|chapter=Taizake Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 5 Yamashiro/Omi|publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022151|ref={{Harvid|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}}} === Related items === * {{jalink|Koryuji|Q3076920}} * {{jalink|Osake Shrine|Q11439397}} (Ako City, Hyogo Prefecture) {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Osake Shinsiya}} == Categories
