Old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = |Shinto Ritual = {{ill|lt=#Festival|UNKNOWN|lt=Cow Festival}} ({{ill|lt=October 12th|Q2920|WD=Q2920|1=October 12}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto city|label=Taizake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} [[File:Osake-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Osake Shrine'' (Osake Jinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kyoto City|Q34600|WD=Q34600|1=Kyoto}}{{ill|lt=Ukyo Ward|Q580186|WD=Q580186|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}}Umasa Hachioka-cho.{{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined God === The following five deities are enshrined. . ; Main enshrined deity :* {{ill|lt=Qin|Q34756|WD=Q34756|1=Qin (state)}}{{ill|lt=First Emperor|Q7192|WD=Q7192|1=Qin Shi Huang}} :* {{ill|lt=Yuzuki-kun|Q11066250|WD=Q11066250|lt=Yuzuki-kun|1=Yuzuki no Kimi}} (Yuzuki no Kimi) :* {{ill|lt=Qin Shugong|Q54867522|WD=Q54867522|1=Sakenokimi Hata}} (Hatanosake no Kimi) ; Aidenjin :* Ehime no Mikoto - Kimono woman. :* Otohime no Mikoto - Han weaver. In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, there is one enshrined deity. In the same book, it is written as ``Osake Shrine {{sub| former name Daisojin}}, so it is known that the original name of the god was ``''Daisojin'''' {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine) | 1979}}.About the god of sake, "Koryuji Raiyuki" (Yamashiro Shukatsu Nogori Kaedeno Oigo-go Koryuji temple history, established in {{ill|lt=Meio|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}}8 ({{ill|lt=1499|Q6708|WD=Q6708|1=1499}})[{{NDLDC|1879799/384}} "Gunshoju Shinkyo Volume 18"] (Naigai Publishing, 1931-1937, National Diet Library Digital Collection) 384-388 frames. is said to be the ancestral god of {{ill|lt=First Emperor|Q7192|WD=Q7192|1=Qin Shi Huang}} of {{ill|lt=Qin|Q34756|WD=Q34756|1=Qin (state)}}, who came to Japan. It is said that a descendant of the Qin Shihuang (Q7192), a distant ancestor of the Qin clan (Q987290), proposed this shrine in 1979. The authenticity of the legend itself is not clear, but as the legend suggests, it is presumed to have been the guardian deity of the Hata clan, which developed the Uzumasa area from ancient times {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}}, and there is also a theory that links the god of sake with Hatanosake no Kimi, who appears in history books. On the other hand, in {{ill|lt=Early modern|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}'s ``{{ill|lt=Yongshufushi|Q18460389|WD=Q18460389|1=Yongshufushi}}'', the enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Monobe Moriya|Q2424736|WD=Q2424736|1=Mononobe no Moriya}}, and one theory says that it was {{ill|lt=Hata Kawakatsu|Q5680877|WD=Q5680877|1=Hata no Kawakatsu}}{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. Also, in "{{ill|lt=Capital Meisho Zue|Q11643495|WD=Q11643495|1=Miyako meisho zue}}", the enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}, {{ill|lt=eight Hatakami|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} Tenma Tenjin, and there are other theories that say it is Qin Shihuang and others that say it is Hata Kawakatsu {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}.In addition to these, recent research has suggested that the original name of the god, ``大辟 (大辟),'' means the so-called {{ill|lt=Dōsojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515|1=Dōsojin}}, which ``avoids (keeps away) disasters.''{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. In this theory, it is pointed out that {{ill|lt=Shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is written as stone in "Kuka Nikkoshu" and "Koryuji Engi" and that many stone deities are generally said to be {{ill|lt=Sartahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahikokami|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}} (Dosojin) {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. On the other hand, there is also a theory that ``Ohosake'' means ``large rift,'' and that it is a deity that honors the Hata clan, who split the land and rivers with their civil engineering skills and cultivated land. 55-59. {{ill|lt=Yoko Sugazawa|Q45819933|WD=Q45819933|1=Yoko Sugasawa}} {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/documents/1296808791808.pdf "Koryuji"]}}'[http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/jinken/yukari06.html Human Rights Inquiring about places of connection to Kyoto City] Kyoto Human Rights Awareness Promotion Council, 2001, pp. 26-27 (link to Kyoto Prefecture homepage). .Although the deity of the ancient enshrined deities is unknown, the above-mentioned five pillars were listed as enshrined deities in the shrine statement book of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}16 ({{ill|lt=1883|Q7816|WD=Q7816|1=1883}}), and they have been inherited to the present day. === History === === Founding === [[File:Kouryuji Roumon.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|{{ill|lt=Koryuji|Q3076920|WD=Q3076920|1=Kōryū-ji}}}}]] Regarding its founding, "Koryu-ji Raiyuki" states that it was founded by the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=King Man|1=UNKNOWN}} (King Mangō}} (first generation of It is said that it began when a descendant of the emperor, Yuzuki-kun's father) prayed to the ``God of the ancestors of the First Qin Emperor.'' Currently, it is enshrined as a small shrine next to the east of {{ill|lt=Koryuji|Q3076920|WD=Q3076920|1=Kōryū-ji}}, but {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} Previously, it was a {{ill|lt=Garanjin|Q10885799|WD=Q10885799|1=Sangharama (Buddhist deity)}} enshrined within the Katsuramiya-in of Koryu-ji Temple. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokushi|1=Rikkokushi}}, {{ill|lt=Kaxiang|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157|1=Kashō}}2 years ({{ill|lt=849 years|Q23902|WD=Q23902|1=849}}) , the ``Daisojin'' of {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Country|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}{{ill|lt=Kazuno District|Q13579879|WD=Q13579879|1=Kadono district}} has a miraculous experience. It is recorded that it was conferred the rank of {{ill|lt=Divine Grade|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}, which is lower than the Jugoi (Junior Fifth Rank) as a shrine {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. {{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" It is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi-sha|1=Shikinaisha}} as the original name of ``Osake-jinja Shrine (Shikiuchi-jinja)'', and it is also stated that it was originally the divine name of ``Osake-jinja Shrine (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}}.In "Koryuji Raiyuki" , it is written that the shrine of ``Osake Daimyojin'' was promoted one after another, and in {{ill|lt=Jireki|Q934365|WD=Q934365|1=Jiryaku}}4 year ({{ill|lt=1068|Q19421|WD=Q19421|1=1068}}) it reached the highest rank of Shoichii {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1979}}. Also, in the same article, it is said that it is one of the 38 shrines that were given to Koryuji during the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=901|Q23580|WD=Q23580|1=901}}-{{ill|lt=923|Q30108|WD=Q30108|1=923}}) as one of the 38 shrines that were designated as shrines to Koryuji. {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} After the "Kuka Nikkoshu" in the Nanbokucho period}}, it appears in literature as the guardian shrine of Katsuragu-in of Koryu-ji Temple, and it moved along with Koryu-ji Temple {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, it was moved to its current location due to {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}. }, {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}} was ranked as {{ill|lt=Murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === God level === * Record of {{ill|lt=Divine rank|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication during the Six Kingdoms historical period ** September 16, 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157|1=Kashō}} ({{ill|lt=849|Q23902|WD=Q23902|1=849}}), Junior Fifth Rank ("{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Koki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}") "Shoku Nihon Koki" Article written on September 16th, Kasho 2nd year (849). - The notation is ``Great God''. * After Six Kingdoms History ** {{ill|lt=Emperor Suzaku|Q361019|WD=Q361019|1=Emperor Suzaku}} years ({{ill|lt=930|Q23409|WD=Q23409|1=930}}-{{ill|lt=946|Q23893|WD=Q23893|1=946}}), Jusanmi (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** May 15, 4th year of {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} ({{ill|lt=941|Q24255|WD=Q24255|1=941}}), Shosanmi (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** November 20, {{ill|lt=Tenki|Q1193591|WD=Q1193591|1=Tengi}}3 years ({{ill|lt=1055 years|Q19371|WD=Q19371|1=1055}}), Shonin 2 (Koryuji Raiyuki) ** April 25, {{ill|lt=Jireki|Q934365|WD=Q934365|1=Jiryaku}}4th year ({{ill|lt=1068|Q19421|WD=Q19421|1=1068}}), Shoichii (Koryuji Raiyuki) === Setsusha === In the "Shikiuchi-sha Investigation Report", the following four shrines are listed as shrines within the precincts. * Kogare Shrine *: The enshrined deity is Okuninushi no Mikoto. "{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsoroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}" {{ill|lt=Jokan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōkan|1=Jōgan}} 4th year ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993|1=862}}) Article {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. “Koryuji Temple Raiyuki” , it appears as ``Kokare Myojin,'' and it is said that when the statue of {{ill|lt=Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773|WD=Q854773|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} was brought to Koryuji Temple, Muko Myojin withered the Japanese japanese tree in front of the gate of Koryuji Temple, but when this deity was enshrined separately as ``Kokare Myojin,'' the tree was revived.{{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}. It is not clear when it was moved to the current precincts of Taizaka Shrine, but it is presumed to have been moved after that time, as it appears in "Yamashiro Meishoshi" that it was enshrined in the rear garden of a private house south of the main gate of Koryuji Temple {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}.*: It is said that the shrine complex at the former enshrinement site of Kogarashi-jinja Shrine was called ``Kogara-no-mori'' (Kogara-no-mori). ``Kokare Mori'' is mentioned in ``Moriwa'' in the 194th paragraph of ``{{ill|lt=Makura Book|Q615137|WD=Q615137|1=The Pillow Book}}'' and is also seen as ``{{ill|lt=utamakura|Q1588579|WD=Q1588579|1=Utamakura}}'' in ``{{ill|lt=later selected Japanese poetry collection|Q1538538|WD=Q1538538|1=Gosen Wakashū}}'', but these are compared to the Kogare Shrine in Yamashiro. There is a theory that compares it to Kokare Shrine in Suruga ({{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}) (see also "{{ill|lt=Kokare Mori|Q60997800|WD=Q60997800|1=Kogashiranomori}}") {{Sfn|Kokare Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}[http://japanknowledge.com/articles/blogjournal/interest_chimei/entry.html?entryid=112 112th Kogare Mori (Kokare Mori)] (Japanese historical place name journal ). . In addition, in "Noin Utamakura" (a poetry book from the middle of the Heian period), both Yamashiro Province and Suruga Province are adopted and listed as Utamakura. * Shrine of the Three Gods - Enshrined Deity: Keirigi no Mikoto * Hachiman Shrine - Enshrined deity: Emperor Ojin * Inazumi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Ukamitama no kami === Festival === [[File:CowFesta.jpg|thumb|280px|right|{{center|"{{ill|lt=Tokyo Famous Places|Q11643495|WD=Q11643495|1=Miyako meisho zue}}" "Uzumasa Cow Festival Pictures"}}]] One of the festivals at Osaka Shrine is known as the ``Ushimatsuri,'' which is held on the night of {{ill|lt=October 12th|Q2920|WD=Q2920|1=October 12}} (previously known as {{ill|lt=September 12th (old calendar)|Q840306|WD=Q840306|lt=September 12th of the lunar calendar|1=September 12 (Lunisolar)}}). {{ill|lt=Nagawa|Q1194173|WD=Q1194173|1=Chōwa}} ({{ill|lt=1012|Q19239|WD=Q19239|1=1012}}-{{ill|lt=1017|Q19249|WD=Q19249|1=1017}}) (Monk)|Q1009263|Genshin}} (Eshin Sozu) is said to have started praying for {{ill|lt=Matarajin|Q6380689|WD=Q6380689|lt=Matarajin|1=Matarajin}} as the guardian deity of Nembutsu, and during the period of syncretism of Shintoism and Buddhism, it was performed by the monks of Koryuji Temple. The Edo period festival is also depicted in ``{{ill|lt=Capital Meisho Zue|Q11643495|WD=Q11643495|1=Miyako meisho zue}}''.During the festival, a person wearing a strange mask who plays the role of Matara-jin rides a bull and parades around the grounds of Koryu-ji Temple accompanied by the Four Heavenly Kings and a procession, and finally reads out the ritual in front of Yakushi-do Hall. This cow festival is counted as one of Kyoto's three strange festivals and is registered as a Kyoto City Registered Intangible Folk Cultural Property. Uzumasa Beef Festival] (Kyoto City homepage). {{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1979}}{{Sfn|Osake Shrine (Gods)|1986}} (Also see "{{ill|lt=Koryuji #Cow Festival|Q3076920|WD=Q3076920|1=Kōryū-ji}}"). === Cultural assets === === Kyoto City registered cultural property === * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Uzumasa Cow Festival - The preservation organization is the Cow Festival Preservation Society.
